skill.name: scientific-schematics
skill.description: Generate publication-ready scientific diagrams from natural language prompts using OpenRouter-hosted image and review models. The skill supports iterative quality review, document-type thresholds, and outputs versioned images plus review logs.
skill.summary: Generate scientific schematics with AI review and refinement.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but most documentation examples and weak-crypto hits are false positives. The confirmed risks are legitimate but important: prompts and generated images are sent to OpenRouter, API keys are read from environment sources, and files are written to user-selected paths. No evidence found of prompt injection attempts or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External AI API Receives User Prompts and Image Data
medium_findings.description: The implementation sends user prompts and generated image data to OpenRouter endpoints for generation and review. This is expected for the skill, but users must avoid submitting confidential research data or regulated information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network request code and review payload construction are explicit. The behavior is legitimate for AI diagram generation, but the privacy impact is real.

medium_findings.title: Environment File Discovery Loads API Key Sources
medium_findings.description: The script searches current, parent, and package directories for .env files, then loads environment variables before reading OPENROUTER_API_KEY. This may unexpectedly use credentials from a parent project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The .env search loop and later API key lookup are direct evidence. The code does not exfiltrate arbitrary .env content, so the finding is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: API Key Forwarded Through Command-Line Arguments
medium_findings.description: The wrapper reads OPENROUTER_API_KEY or --api-key and appends the key to the child Python command. Command-line arguments can be exposed through local process inspection on some systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper explicitly appends --api-key to the argv list when a key is present. Exposure depends on local operating-system visibility and runtime context.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output Paths Write Images and Logs
medium_findings.description: The generator creates directories, writes versioned images, copies the final output, and writes a review log under the requested output path. This is intended functionality, but it can overwrite or create files where the caller has permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes and directory creation are clear in the implementation. Risk is bounded by local user permissions and the caller-selected output path.

low_findings.title: Subprocess Execution Uses Argument List Rather Than Shell
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged command execution, but the wrapper uses subprocess.run with an argv list and invokes a local Python script. This reduces shell-injection risk, though the skill still requires Bash and script execution permissions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct evidence, and there is no shell=True usage in the inspected wrapper. User input is passed as an argument to the child script, not interpolated into a shell string.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Triggered Many False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static command findings are fenced documentation examples for image conversion, inspection, installation, or skill usage. They are not executed automatically by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples, not executable code paths. They can still influence user behavior, but they do not prove malicious automation.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decoding Is Used for Image Handling
low_findings.description: The base64 decoding findings appear tied to extracting image bytes from API responses and data URLs. No evidence found that base64 is used to hide commands or payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decode calls operate on image data returned by the API. I did not find evidence of decoded code execution or hidden command payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated HTTP POST to External Service
dangerous_patterns.description: The script constructs an Authorization header with the OpenRouter API key and posts prompt payloads to OpenRouter. This is core functionality but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and requests.post call are explicit. The destination is the configured OpenRouter base URL.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Parent Directory Environment Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The loader searches upward through parent directories for .env files and loads them without overriding existing variables. This can surprise users in nested workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upward .env traversal is present and bounded to five levels. It is credential-related but not clearly malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Creation From User Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The generator creates output directories and writes generated images plus review logs based on the requested path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are direct and intentional. The security impact depends on the user-provided path and local permissions.

content.value_statement: Scientific figures take time to draft, review, and polish for publication. This skill turns detailed diagram prompts into generated images with automated quality review and refinement.
actual_capabilities: Generates scientific diagram images from natural language descriptions.
Uses document-type quality thresholds for journals, posters, presentations, and reports.
Reviews generated diagrams with a vision model and records scores and critiques.
Creates versioned image files for each iteration and a final copied output file.
Writes a review log that summarizes prompts, scores, critiques, and early stopping.
Supports command-line options for output path, document type, iterations, API key, and verbose mode.
limitations: Requires an OpenRouter API key and network access to generate or review diagrams.
Generated figures may contain scientific or text errors and require expert review.
Outputs are raster images, not editable vector diagrams.
The scripts write files to user-provided output paths and should be run in a trusted workspace.

use_cases.title: Draft Journal Figures
use_cases.description: Create first-pass CONSORT, PRISMA, pathway, or architecture diagrams for manuscript review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate clear schematics for lectures, workshops, and internal training materials.

use_cases.title: Prototype Technical Diagrams
use_cases.description: Explore system architecture, model architecture, and workflow layouts before final design work.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Diagram
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a simple schematic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a clean scientific diagram showing [process or system]. Include the main components, labels, and arrows showing flow.

prompt_templates.title: Publication Figure
prompt_templates.scenario: Add publication context and layout requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a journal-ready [diagram type] for [topic]. Use a light background, readable labels, colorblind-friendly colors, and a left-to-right layout.

prompt_templates.title: Structured Research Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Request specific quantities and review criteria.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a [CONSORT or PRISMA] flow diagram with these counts: [counts]. Use document type [journal, poster, or presentation] and preserve all labels.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Iterative Figure
prompt_templates.scenario: Use the full refinement workflow for a complex diagram.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a complex [pathway, model architecture, or system diagram] with [components]. Use two iterations, document type [type], and save to [output path].

output_examples.input: A CONSORT participant flow diagram for a randomized trial with screening, exclusions, randomization, follow-up, and analysis counts.
output_examples.output: A versioned participant flow diagram, a final image file, and a review log with quality score, critique, and acceptance status.

output_examples.input: A transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention blocks, embeddings, and cross-attention connections.
output_examples.output: A labeled neural network architecture image that shows the requested blocks, flow direction, and review feedback for clarity.

output_examples.input: A MAPK signaling pathway from receptor activation to transcriptional response.
output_examples.output: A pathway schematic with labeled signaling components, directional arrows, color guidance, and a model-generated quality review.

best_practices: Run the skill only in projects where external AI processing of prompts and images is acceptable.
Use specific prompts with required labels, counts, directions, and target document type.
Review every generated figure for scientific accuracy before publication or submission.
anti_patterns: Do not include private patient data, unpublished sensitive data, or secrets in diagram prompts.
Do not store API keys permanently in shared shell profiles or committed environment files.
Do not treat generated review scores as a substitute for subject-matter review.

faq.question: Does this skill require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The generation and review scripts call the OpenRouter API for image generation and quality review.

faq.question: Which API key is required?
faq.answer: The scripts require an OpenRouter API key through OPENROUTER_API_KEY or an explicit command-line option.

faq.question: Can it create editable vector diagrams?
faq.answer: No. The implementation saves generated raster image files and review logs.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is the output ready for direct publication?
faq.answer: No. The output may be publication-oriented, but users must verify labels, science, accessibility, and journal requirements.

faq.question: Where are files written?
faq.answer: The scripts write versioned images, a final image, and a review log under the user-provided output path.
